claims.text: Mel Waters discovered a nine-foot-wide bottomless hole on his property in Manastash Ridge west of Ellensburg, Washington, that reaches at least 80,000 feet (15+ miles) deep.

evidence.quote:
l ran
out of line he was eighty thousand feet
down over 15 miles and still no bottom
while experimenting Mel notice

claims.rationale: No such hole has ever been independently verified. A ~24 km vertical hole in continental crust is not geologically possible — the deepest human-drilled hole (Kola Superdeep, ~12.3 km) required a decade of engineering. The '80,000 feet of fishing line' measurement is an unwitnessed claim by a pseudonymous caller with no photographs or coordinates released. The host himself states the hole 'just isn't geologically possible'.

claims.text: The hole itself just isn't geologically possible — a hole many miles deep would be unstable and collapse in on itself.

evidence.quote:
n't do it now
the hole itself just isn't geologically
possible a whole many miles deep would
be unstable and collapse in on itself
unless it

claims.rationale: Correct geophysics. Rock strength and lithostatic pressure make open vertical cavities more than ~3-5 km deep impossible in crystalline crust without engineered lining. This is a textbook constraint from mining and petroleum engineering (e.g. ZoBack, Reservoir Geomechanics).

dependencies.rationale: [logical] The geological impossibility of a 15-mile open hole falsifies the central premise of the Mel's Hole story.

claims.text: Mel Waters sent Art Bell a message on February 21st 1997, and the call launched Mel's Hole as a nationally known mystery on Coast to Coast AM.

evidence.quote:
nce Mel
sent art a message on February 21st 1997
art called him and Mel's Hall became
known to the world over

claims.rationale: The February 21, 1997 Coast-to-Coast AM broadcast featuring 'Mel Waters' and his alleged hole is a well-documented radio episode. Transcripts, tape trades, and show archives confirm the date. This event is real as a media artifact — the content of Mel's claims is separate (see other claims).

claims.text: Coast to Coast AM was broadcast on hundreds of stations around the world and had approximately 10 million listeners.

evidence.quote:
ss this great land of
ours Coast to Coast was on hundreds of
stations around the world and had 10
million listeners if anyone

claims.rationale: Coast to Coast AM in the late 1990s did carry on ~500+ affiliates and reached roughly 10 million listeners per week by Premiere Networks' reporting. 'Hundreds of stations' and '10 million' are broadly consistent with the public record.

claims.text: After Mel appeared on the Art Bell show, armed military personnel blocked his property and evidence showed heavy machinery had been brought in.

evidence.quote:
property was blocked by armed
military personnel and there was
evidence that heavy machinery had been
brought in M

claims.rationale: There is no public record of any US military operation near Ellensburg matching this description — no FOIA filings, no county records, no local news coverage at the time. Military property seizures in the continental US leave paper trails. Near-zero directTruth as a real event.

dependencies.rationale: Government-coverup element specific to the Mel's Hole narrative.

claims.text: Mel's radio picked up a baseball game that was played in 1967, thirty years earlier.

evidence.quote:
e learns the game was played in
1967. 30 years ago Art's first call with
Mel goes on for about an hour and then
they wra

claims.rationale: Radios receiving ancient broadcasts is physically impossible — electromagnetic waves propagate at light speed and dissipate; there's no mechanism to store and rebroadcast a 1967 baseball game. The claim is offered as-is with no verification of the specific call or station.

dependencies.rationale: Paranormal-electromagnetic element of the hole's alleged properties.

claims.text: There's no record of a Mel or Melvin Waters living in the Ellensburg area, though some claim he used a pseudonym.

evidence.quote:
a complicated
character there's no record of a male or
Melvin Waters living in the area but
some have claimed he was using a
pseu

claims.rationale: Multiple researchers (including Red Elk skeptics and investigative journalists) have tried to trace 'Mel Waters' via Kittitas County records, property tax rolls, and voter rolls without success. The lack-of-record finding is a strong anonymity signal consistent with a pseudonymous caller.

dependencies.rationale: [undercuts] A caller who cannot be traced in public records weakens every first-person claim he makes about the hole.

claims.text: Despite years of searching by listeners and researchers, Mel's Hole has never been found.

evidence.quote:
d Mel's home they
haven't it's never been found at least
not yet as for Mel he's a complicated
character there's no reco

claims.rationale: Accurate as of the video's publication. Multiple amateur and semi-professional expeditions (documented on paranormal blogs and in Peter Huston's skeptic literature) have searched Manastash Ridge since 1997 without locating a matching feature.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Twenty-five years of unsuccessful searches is strong evidence the hole as described doesn't exist.

claims.text: A Native American tribe brought Mel to a second bottomless hole in Nevada on public land used by Basque shepherds, who considered it sacred for at least 200 years.

evidence.quote:
public land that was
used by Basques the baskar and ethnic
group that comes from a small region
between France and Spain The Basq

claims.rationale: The Basque community in Nevada is real (sheep herding from the 1850s-present in Elko and Humboldt counties). The second-hole story has no documentation beyond Mel's radio appearances; no Basque community or tribal organization has corroborated it. No basis for directTruth on the hole's existence.

dependencies.rationale: Second-hole discovery expands the original hole thesis into a broader phenomenon.

claims.text: Ice lowered into the Nevada hole came back dry, transformed into salt-like material that caught fire when exposed to flame and burned for months.

evidence.quote:
it didn't melt
did it nope it caught fire not only that
it continued to burn for months so they
continued sending diffe

claims.rationale: Ice catching fire and burning for months violates chemistry (water is fire-retardant, not fuel). No sample of this 'transformed ice' has ever been produced for laboratory analysis. The claim has no physical mechanism and no corroborating evidence.

dependencies.rationale: Specific paranormal phenomenon tied to the Nevada hole.

claims.text: A sheep lowered into the Nevada hole came back dead with a giant tumor containing what Mel described as a fetal seal with human eyes, attached by umbilical cord.

evidence.quote:
tumor is what Mel
describes as
fetal seal wait wait did you just say a
fetal seal yeah like a baby seal it was
attached to the tumor by an

claims.rationale: A sheep developing a 'seal-with-human-eyes' inside a tumor in 30 minutes violates every principle of developmental biology. No specimen, no autopsy photo, no veterinary report. Extraordinary claim with no evidence.

dependencies.rationale: Most famous single episode of the Mel's Hole canon.

claims.text: Before going to Nevada Mel had advanced esophageal cancer with six months to live; after the hole experience he was cancer-free and believes the 'magic seal' cured him.

evidence.quote:
s
diagnosed with Advanced esophageal
cancer he had only six months to live
but after this experience Mel was
cancer-free he thinks he was cured by
the

claims.rationale: Advanced (stage IV) esophageal cancer in 1997 had a ~5-10% 5-year survival rate with treatment and <5% without. 'Cured by magic seal' is not a verifiable medical claim — no diagnosis records, no imaging, no oncologist statement. Without any medical provenance, near-zero directTruth.

dependencies.rationale: The healing claim depends on the fetal-seal encounter being real.

claims.text: Credibility of Mel's story is hurt by the lack of pictures — Mel never produces photographs, recordings, or physical evidence despite repeated requests from Art Bell.

evidence.quote:
edibility of the story is
the lack of pictures in every call Art
asks for photographs but sometimes Mel
just doesn't think to take

claims.rationale: Host correctly identifies the epistemic core weakness. Physical evidence is repeatedly promised and never delivered — a classic tell for fabricated or embellished testimony, especially over five years of calls.

dependencies.rationale: [undercuts] Total absence of physical evidence after years of requests undermines every substantive claim in the Mel's Hole canon.

claims.text: Art Bell treated the Mel's Hole story as entertainment rather than investigation — 'hoax or not, it's a great story' — and let Mel continue without fact-checking.

evidence.quote:
that this
is all a hoax but Art Bell is the master
hoax or not it's a great story so he
lets Mel continue but art d

claims.rationale: This is the host's characterization of Bell's on-air approach, but Bell's entertainment-first framing is well-documented and consistent with his editorial style on other anomaly segments. Accurate take on a veteran broadcaster's method — but an editorial-opinion claim that doesn't warrant a firm directTruth number.

claims.text: Terra Server launched only six months after Mel's first phone call, so Mel could not have known in advance which government maps would black out the area.

evidence.quote:
there the problem with that
is Terra server launched six months
after Mel made the first phone call he
couldn't have known so

claims.rationale: TerraServer-USA launched in June 1998 (Microsoft Research partnership), roughly 15 months after Mel's Feb 1997 call — the '6 months' timing is close but not exact. The logical point stands: pre-existing USGS aerial imagery did exist, so 'Mel couldn't have known what would be blacked out' is weaker than the host implies. No directTruth because the specific timing is slightly off and the logical conclusion is partly undermined.

dependencies.rationale: Cited as partial defense of Mel's credibility against the pre-selected-location-from-maps critique.
